content.text: Why Integration Milestones Fail in GenAI Programmes

GenAI initiatives span teams that operate on very different cadences: ML Engineering measures progress in model evaluation runs (days), Data Platform tracks pipeline reliability (weekly), and Product ships UX in two-week sprints. Without explicit
that force all teams to converge on a shared deliverable at a fixed date, each team optimises locally and the integration point arrives as a surprise.

The most common delivery failure pattern: every team finishes their slice on time, but nobody's slice connects to anyone else's at the integration point. The cost is paid in crunch weeks of rework that nobody budgeted.

Integration milestone coordination is the practice of defining those convergence points explicitly—what each team must deliver, how readiness is verified, who owns the gate decision, and what happens when a team is late.

attrs.alt: Integration milestone timeline showing four team swimlanes converging at IM-1 (W9: Integration Ready) and IM-2 (W15: Release Ready)
attrs.title: Integration Milestone Timeline

content.text: The timeline shows four teams (ML Engineering, Data Platform, Platform/Infra, Product/UX) running parallel workstreams. Each team owns upstream milestones (M1–M4) within their lane. At W9, all four streams converge at
—the first cross-team gate. Shared work (SIT, E2E validation, load testing, UAT) runs W9–W15, closing at
Each integration milestone is a first-class object with explicit inputs from every contributing team, a clear readiness criteria, and a designated owner who makes the go/no-go call.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

class MilestoneStatus(Enum):
    PLANNED   = "planned"
    AT_RISK   = "at_risk"
    BLOCKED   = "blocked"
    ACHIEVED  = "achieved"
    MISSED    = "missed"

class ReadinessSignal(Enum):
    GREEN  = "green"   # committed, on track
    AMBER  = "amber"   # at risk, mitigation in place
    RED    = "red"     # will miss, no mitigation yet

@dataclass
class TeamReadiness:
    team: str
    deliverable: str            # what this team must hand off
    target_date: date
    signal: ReadinessSignal
    note: str = ""
    actual_date: Optional[date] = None

    def is_late(self, as_of: date) -> bool:
        return self.signal != ReadinessSignal.GREEN and as_of > self.target_date

@dataclass
class IntegrationMilestone:
    id: str                         # e.g. "IM-1"
    title: str
    target_date: date
    owner: str                      # role who calls go/no-go
    inputs: list[TeamReadiness] = field(default_factory=list)
    status: MilestoneStatus = MilestoneStatus.PLANNED
    gate_criteria: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    actual_date: Optional[date] = None

    def readiness_summary(self) -> dict[str, ReadinessSignal]:
        return {r.team: r.signal for r in self.inputs}

    def overall_rag(self) -> ReadinessSignal:
        signals = [r.signal for r in self.inputs]
        if any(s == ReadinessSignal.RED for s in signals):
            return ReadinessSignal.RED
        if any(s == ReadinessSignal.AMBER for s in signals):
            return ReadinessSignal.AMBER
        return ReadinessSignal.GREEN

    def blocking_teams(self, as_of: date) -> list[TeamReadiness]:
        return [r for r in self.inputs if r.is_late(as_of)]
Vague gate criteria ('feature complete', 'model ready') are the root cause of disputes at integration points. Each gate criterion must be
—it either passes or it does not, with no room for interpretation.
Model evaluation score ≥ 85% on held-out set
Inference latency p99 < 300 ms at 500 RPS
Data pipeline SLA: < 0.1% row loss over 7 days
UX prototype accepted by product owner

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable, Any

@dataclass
class GateCriterion:
    id: str
    description: str
    owner_team: str
    verify: Callable[[], tuple[bool, str]]  # returns (passed, evidence_url)

    def evaluate(self) -> dict:
        passed, evidence = self.verify()
        return {
            "criterion": self.id,
            "description": self.description,
            "owner": self.owner_team,
            "passed": passed,
            "evidence": evidence,
        }


def gate_check(milestone: "IntegrationMilestone", criteria: list[GateCriterion]) -> dict:
    """
    Run all gate criteria and return a go/no-go decision with per-criterion details.
    All criteria must pass for a GO decision.
    """
    results = [c.evaluate() for c in criteria]
    all_passed = all(r["passed"] for r in results)
    blockers = [r for r in results if not r["passed"]]
    return {
        "milestone_id": milestone.id,
        "decision": "GO" if all_passed else "NO-GO",
        "blockers": blockers,
        "details": results,
    }


# Example criteria for IM-1
def check_model_eval() -> tuple[bool, str]:
    score = 0.87  # fetched from eval store
    return score >= 0.85, f"eval_score={score}"

def check_latency() -> tuple[bool, str]:
    p99_ms = 275  # fetched from Grafana
    return p99_ms < 300, f"p99={p99_ms}ms"

IM1_CRITERIA = [
    GateCriterion("C-ML-01", "Model eval ≥ 85%", "ML Engineering", check_model_eval),
    GateCriterion("C-PLAT-01", "Latency p99 < 300ms", "Platform/Infra", check_latency),
]
Integration milestones require a dedicated coordination cadence
from team-level standups. The standard pattern runs three meetings per milestone:
(T−6 weeks): agree gate criteria, assign owners, lock target date. Document outputs in the Integration Milestone Brief.
(T−3 weeks): each team reports RAG signal for their deliverable. First opportunity to surface AMBER/RED and trigger mitigations before it is too late to recover.
(T−1 week): final RAG assessment. Go/no-go decision is made by the milestone owner based on gate criterion evidence. Escalation to programme board if any RED exists.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum

class SyncType(Enum):
    KICKOFF     = "kickoff"
    MIDPOINT    = "midpoint"
    READINESS   = "readiness_review"

@dataclass
class MilestoneSyncEvent:
    sync_type: SyncType
    scheduled_date: date
    agenda_items: list[str]
    required_attendees: list[str]   # team lead from each contributing team
    outputs: list[str]              # what must be produced by end of meeting

def build_sync_calendar(milestone_date: date) -> list[MilestoneSyncEvent]:
    return [
        MilestoneSyncEvent(
            SyncType.KICKOFF,
            milestone_date - timedelta(weeks=6),
            agenda_items=[
                "Review and agree gate criteria for each team",
                "Assign criterion owners and verification methods",
                "Confirm target date and discuss risk scenarios",
                "Document Integration Milestone Brief",
            ],
            required_attendees=["ML Lead", "Data Platform Lead", "Platform Lead", "Product Lead"],
            outputs=["Signed Integration Milestone Brief", "Risk register entries"],
        ),
        MilestoneSyncEvent(
            SyncType.MIDPOINT,
            milestone_date - timedelta(weeks=3),
            agenda_items=[
                "Each team: RAG signal + one-sentence status",
                "Review AMBER/RED items: mitigation or escalation?",
                "Update risk register",
                "Decide if date adjustment is needed",
            ],
            required_attendees=["ML Lead", "Data Platform Lead", "Platform Lead", "Product Lead", "PM"],
            outputs=["Updated RAG dashboard", "Mitigation actions with owners and dates"],
        ),
        MilestoneSyncEvent(
            SyncType.READINESS,
            milestone_date - timedelta(weeks=1),
            agenda_items=[
                "Verify each gate criterion with evidence",
                "Formal go/no-go decision by milestone owner",
                "If NO-GO: agree revised date and escalation path",
                "Confirm joint-work schedule for post-IM period",
            ],
            required_attendees=["ML Lead", "Data Platform Lead", "Platform Lead", "Product Lead", "PM", "Delivery Director"],
            outputs=["Go/No-Go decision record", "Revised plan if NO-GO"],
        ),
    ]
When one team signals RED at the mid-point or readiness review, the milestone owner must choose from three options:
(integration continues with a known gap, gap closed before IM-2), or
(push the integration milestone with a revised plan).

from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

class LateTeamResponse(Enum):
    ABSORB          = "absorb"           # all teams wait
    PARTIAL_PROCEED = "partial_proceed"  # proceed with known gap
    DATE_SLIP       = "date_slip"        # push milestone

@dataclass
class LateTeamDecision:
    milestone_id: str
    late_team: str
    response: LateTeamResponse
    rationale: str
    revised_date: date | None = None      # only if DATE_SLIP
    gap_closure_date: date | None = None  # only if PARTIAL_PROCEED
    decision_owner: str = ""

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        errors = []
        if self.response == LateTeamResponse.DATE_SLIP and self.revised_date is None:
            errors.append("DATE_SLIP requires a revised_date")
        if self.response == LateTeamResponse.PARTIAL_PROCEED and self.gap_closure_date is None:
            errors.append("PARTIAL_PROCEED requires a gap_closure_date")
        return errors


def decide_late_team_response(
    late_team: TeamReadiness,
    milestone: IntegrationMilestone,
    days_late: int,
    gap_is_isolatable: bool,
) -> LateTeamResponse:
    """
    Heuristic decision tree. In practice the milestone owner makes the call
    with full stakeholder context — this logic structures the conversation.
    """
    if days_late <= 3 and gap_is_isolatable:
        return LateTeamResponse.PARTIAL_PROCEED
    elif days_late <= 7:
        return LateTeamResponse.ABSORB
    else:
        return LateTeamResponse.DATE_SLIP
Track milestone health across the programme portfolio using a simple scorecard. Each integration milestone gets a row; the score drives escalation and retrospective focus.
Milestone date set top-down without team input

: teams agree to a date they privately know is impossible, then say nothing until the readiness review. Fix: teams estimate their deliverable date independently; the PM finds the bottleneck and negotiates.
Gate criteria defined the day before the review
: criteria are only meaningful if teams have had weeks to design toward them. Criteria must be locked at kick-off or they are meaningless checkboxes.
: IM-1 means "all inputs are ready for integration work to begin", not "the system is live". Conflating the two produces scope creep inside the integration window.
: treating a late team as an all-or-nothing blocker leads to unnecessary date slips. Many gaps can be isolated and covered with a stub or mock while the late team catches up.
Readiness review as a status report, not a decision
: the review must end with an explicit go/no-go. If the meeting ends with "let's see", the milestone has effectively slipped without a plan.

After each integration milestone, a 30-minute retrospective should capture three numbers: actual vs. target date, number of gate criteria that required rework, and which team's readiness signal was least accurate. These feed the next milestone's risk model.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class MilestoneRetro:
    milestone_id: str
    target_date: date
    actual_date: date
    criteria_reworked: int       # gate criteria that failed initially
    least_accurate_signal: str   # team whose RAG was most misleading
    root_cause: str
    action_items: list[str]

    @property
    def slip_days(self) -> int:
        return max(0, (self.actual_date - self.target_date).days)

    def formatted(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"Milestone: {self.milestone_id}",
            f"Slip: {self.slip_days} days",
            f"Criteria reworked: {self.criteria_reworked}",
            f"Least accurate signal: {self.least_accurate_signal}",
            f"Root cause: {self.root_cause}",
            "Actions:",
        ] + [f"  - {a}" for a in self.action_items]
        return "\n".join(lines)


# Example
retro = MilestoneRetro(
    milestone_id="IM-1",
    target_date=date(2025, 3, 7),
    actual_date=date(2025, 3, 12),
    criteria_reworked=2,
    least_accurate_signal="Data Platform (AMBER → RED day before review)",
    root_cause="Data quality gate was not automated; manual checks discovered issues late",
    action_items=[
        "Automate row-loss check in CI pipeline before next milestone",
        "Require Data Platform to surface RED signals ≥ 5 days before readiness review",
        "Add data quality criterion to IM-2 kick-off brief",
    ],
)
print(retro.formatted())
Integration milestone coordination is the connective tissue between team-level delivery and programme-level outcomes. Effective programmes treat integration milestones as

: they have explicit gate criteria agreed at kick-off, a three-sync coordination cadence, a named go/no-go owner, and a structured response for when a contributing team is late. The scorecard and retrospective close the loop so that each successive milestone is better forecast than the last.
